title: Shortbread fingers
subtitle: Pressed-and-baked all-butter shortbread
excerpt: Traditional Scottish shortbread pressed into a tin, scored into fingers, and baked low until pale gold. The ratio is one of sugar to two of butter to three of flour, by weight; everything else is detail.
sourceNotes: F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): documents the canonical 1:2:3 shortbread ratio (sugar:butter:flour by weight). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the 'Scotch shortbread' recipe gives the pressed-tin method. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the fingers shape is documented as a household biscuit-tin standard, distinct from petticoat tails (round, wedge-cut).

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool fully, freeze in a tin or bag for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly into a larger tin or two square tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Press into the tin and chill for an hour or up to a day before baking; the colder the dough, the cleaner the fingers cut.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — low and slow keeps the shortbread pale

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm square, lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: Shortbread is baked at a low oven temperature (150°C fan) for a long time (40–45 minutes) so the biscuit dries through without browning. The flavour wants pale-gold; a deep tan reads as overdone and the texture goes hard rather than crumbly. The colour is the cue, not the time.

content.text: Shortbread is the simplest of all baked things: three ingredients, one ratio, one method. By weight, one part sugar to two parts butter to three parts flour. The flour is plain; the sugar is caster (granulated leaves a grainy crumb); the butter is unsalted and good. Everything else is variation.

The fingers are pressed into a square tin, scored before baking so they break cleanly, and baked low and long until pale gold all over. Dust with caster sugar straight from the oven while the surface is still warm.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a bowl until pale and just creamed; a minute by hand or thirty seconds in a mixer. Do not over-aerate. Sift in the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and bring together by hand into a soft, even dough.

Tip the dough into the tin and press evenly across the base with the heel of your hand. Level the top with a palette knife or the back of a metal spoon. Score the surface into twenty fingers (four across, five down) with a sharp knife, going about halfway through. Prick each finger twice with a fork. Chill in the fridge for at least 30 minutes — overnight is fine.

for 40–45 minutes; the shortbread should be pale gold all over, not browned. Take from the oven and dust the surface with the reserved caster sugar while still warm. Re-score the fingers along the original lines, cutting all the way through. Cool in the tin until firm enough to lift, then transfer to a rack.
